writing_type: personal food and travel blog posts
user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, first-person narrative essays that drop readers into a specific moment through highly personal sensory detail and embodied experience. Values a conversational, self-aware voice that never over-explains, and structures sections to lean forward into what's next rather than resolve neatly.

rubric.name: Sensory Specificity & Authenticity
rubric.description: Sensory details must be specific, unexpected, and personal — the kind only someone who was actually there could write. Generic category-level descriptions ('pork bones and soy') fail; the goal is the weird, true detail (cedar in a sauna) that makes a reader believe you.

dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions go beyond naming ingredients or categories to capture something unexpected or hard to articulate
At least one sensory detail is anchored to a personal comparison or memory that only this writer would reach for
No sensory language reads as stock or predictable — nothing that could have been written without being there
Details engage more than one sense and layer them (e.g., smell described through warmth, texture, sharpness)

rubric.name: Embodied Immediacy
rubric.description: Writing renders experience as felt in the body, not observed from a distance. The reader should feel the physical jolt — what the mouth does, what the chest does, what the feet do — rather than being told that something was powerful or beautiful.

dimensions.label: At least one moment captures an involuntary physical reaction (mouth watering, feet stopping, breath catching)
Intensity is conveyed through physical rendering, not through adjectives or claims about how powerful something was
Key moments unfold with a present-tense, you-are-here quality even if written in past tense
The body's response precedes or overrides conscious thought — experience lands before interpretation

rubric.name: Conversational Personal Voice
rubric.description: Tone is first-person, warm, and conversational — like talking to a friend who happens to be a great storyteller. Humor is understated and self-aware, never performative. Sentence rhythms feel like natural thought, including fragments, asides, and self-corrections.

dimensions.label: Sentences vary in length and structure, including fragments and trailing constructions that mimic natural thought
Humor is self-deprecating or observational and never calls attention to itself as a joke
Includes casual asides or parenthetical thoughts that feel like the writer thinking in real time
No line feels like it's trying too hard to be quotable, poetic, or writerly

rubric.name: Section Architecture & Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Sections bridge forward rather than conclude. Endings should leave the reader leaning in — feeling that something is about to happen — not settling back. Transitions between moments are smooth and cinematic, with no jarring cuts that skip over how the writer got from one beat to the next.

dimensions.label: The section's final beat creates anticipation or unresolved tension rather than wrapping things up
Movement between scenes or moments feels continuous — no cuts that leave the reader wondering what happened in between
Transitions carry emotional or sensory continuity even when time or place shifts
Reflective or philosophical lines don't resolve the section's energy — they open questions rather than answer them

rubric.name: Restraint & Trust in the Reader
rubric.description: The writing trusts readers to feel what's there without over-explaining or narrating every connection. The best lines land the reader in a moment and leave them there. Aphoristic or reflective lines earn their place through what precedes them, not through their own prettiness.

dimensions.label: Key moments are rendered in scene rather than summarized or explained after the fact
Any reflective or philosophical line is grounded in a specific preceding experience — it doesn't float
The writing avoids tying things up too tidily — it's comfortable with ambiguity, not-knowing, or the unexplained
Does not tell the reader how to feel or flag the significance of a moment — lets the detail do the work

coaching_notes: This user has a strong internal compass for what feels authentic vs. generic — they can diagnose problems precisely ('it's telling me it was powerful without making me feel the power') but sometimes need help generating the fix. Push them toward their own weird, specific memories rather than offering polished alternatives. They think about structure cinematically (cuts, establishing shots, bridges) so use that vocabulary. Non-negotiable: never let a sensory detail stay at the category level — always ask 'what did it actually remind you of?'

user_goals_summary: The user writes immersive, first-person food and travel narratives that drop readers into specific moments through unexpected sensory detail and physical immediacy. They value a warm, conversational voice with understated humor, and care deeply about structural flow — sections should bridge forward with no jarring cuts, and endings should leave the reader leaning in rather than settling back.

rubric.name: Sensory Specificity & Authenticity
rubric.description: Sensory details must be specific, unexpected, and personal — the kind only someone who was actually there could write. Generic category-level descriptions fail; the goal is the weird, true detail (cedar in a sauna, the bottom of a cast iron pan) that makes a reader believe you.

dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions go beyond naming ingredients or categories to capture something unexpected or hard to articulate
At least one sensory detail is anchored to a personal comparison or memory that only this writer would reach for
No sensory language reads as stock or predictable — nothing that could have been written without being there
Details engage more than one sense and layer them (e.g., smell described through warmth, texture, sharpness)

rubric.name: Embodied Immediacy
rubric.description: Writing renders experience as felt in the body, not observed from a distance. The reader should feel the physical jolt — what the mouth does, what the shoulders do, what the feet do — rather than being told that something was powerful or beautiful. This is the kind of physical detail the user always wants more of.

dimensions.label: At least one moment captures an involuntary physical reaction (mouth watering, feet stopping, shoulders dropping)
Intensity is conveyed through physical rendering, not through adjectives or claims about how powerful something was
Key moments unfold with a present-tense, you-are-here quality even if written in past tense
The body's response precedes or overrides conscious thought — experience lands before interpretation

rubric.name: Section Architecture & Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Sections bridge forward rather than conclude. Endings leave the reader leaning in, not settling back. Transitions are smooth and cinematic with no jarring cuts, and sequential observations within a section resonate as variations on a shared idea rather than sitting as an unconnected list.

dimensions.label: The section's final beat creates anticipation or lands the reader in an unresolved moment rather than wrapping things up
Movement between scenes or moments feels continuous — no cuts that leave the reader wondering what happened in between
Brief moments of backstory or context are woven in to make immersive moments feel earned rather than arbitrary
Sequential observations within a section resonate thematically — they build on a shared idea rather than sitting as an unconnected list
Reflective or philosophical lines don't resolve the section's energy — they open questions rather than answer them

rubric.name: Conversational Personal Voice
rubric.description: Tone is first-person, warm, and conversational — like talking to a friend who happens to be a great storyteller. Humor is understated and self-aware, never performative. Sentence rhythms feel like natural thought, including fragments, asides, and self-corrections.

dimensions.label: Sentences vary in length and structure, including fragments and trailing constructions that mimic natural thought
Humor is self-deprecating or observational and never calls attention to itself as a joke
Includes casual asides or parenthetical thoughts that feel like the writer thinking in real time
No line feels like it's trying too hard to be quotable, poetic, or writerly

rubric.name: Restraint & Trust in the Reader
rubric.description: The writing trusts readers to feel what's there without over-explaining or narrating every connection. The best lines land the reader in a moment and leave them there. Reflective lines earn their place through what precedes them, not through their own prettiness.

dimensions.label: Key moments are rendered in scene rather than summarized or explained after the fact
Any reflective or philosophical line is grounded in a specific preceding experience — it doesn't float
The writing avoids tying things up too tidily — comfortable with ambiguity, not-knowing, or the unexplained
Does not tell the reader how to feel or flag the significance of a moment — lets the detail do the work

coaching_notes: This user diagnoses structural problems with remarkable precision ('it floats,' 'ran out of steam,' 'started mid-sentence') but looks to the collaborator for solutions — offer multiple options and let them choose. Voice and sensory instincts are strong and arrive early; structure is where the real revision work happens, especially endings and transitions. Non-negotiable: sensory details must feel like only this writer could have written them — 'accurate' is not enough, it must be 'weird and true.'

writing_type: personal food and travel narrative blog posts

Their writing earns emotional weight through restraint and detail, not through reflection or explanation.

Sequential observations within a section resonate thematically — they build on a shared idea rather than sitting as an unconnected list

rubric.name: Restraint & Trust in the Reader
rubric.description: The writing stays in the room rather than summarizing the room. The best lines land the reader in a physical moment and leave them there. Meaning is conveyed through small actions and details, not through the narrator explaining what something meant. Endings stay present, not reflective.

dimensions.label: Key moments stay in the physical scene — the writing ends in the room, not in the narrator's head reflecting on the room
Any reflective or philosophical line is grounded in a specific preceding experience — it doesn't float
The writing avoids tying things up too tidily — comfortable with ambiguity, not-knowing, or the unexplained
Does not tell the reader how to feel or flag the significance of a moment — lets the detail do the work
Small actions or nonverbal moments carry emotional weight without being explained or annotated

rubric.name: Conversational Personal Voice
rubric.description: Tone is first-person, warm, and conversational — like talking to a friend who happens to be a great storyteller. Humor is understated and self-aware, never performative. Sentence rhythms feel like natural thought, including fragments, asides, and self-corrections.

dimensions.label: Sentences vary in length and structure, including fragments and trailing constructions that mimic natural thought
Humor is self-deprecating or observational and never calls attention to itself as a joke
Includes casual asides or parenthetical thoughts that feel like the writer thinking in real time
No line feels like it's trying too hard to be quotable, poetic, or writerly

coaching_notes: The user's #1 revision pattern across every iteration is endings that resolve or reflect too much — they will consistently push to stay in the physical scene rather than retreat into the narrator's head. They will always choose the weird, specific, personal sensory detail over the accurate-but-expected one; if a description could have been written without being there, it needs replacing. Humor should sit in the punctuation and the asides — parenthetical self-deprecation, not setups and punchlines.

writing_type: personal travel and food writing (blog posts)
user_goals_summary: Writes immersive, first-person scene-driven posts that drop readers into a physical moment through layered sensory detail and embodied experience. Values writing that feels lived-in and unreplicable — the kind of specificity that proves you were there. Sections should lean forward rather than resolve, with smooth cinematic transitions and endings that leave readers in the room, not reflecting on it.

rubric.name: Conversational Personal Voice
rubric.description: Tone is first-person, warm, and conversational — like talking to a friend who happens to be a great storyteller. Humor is understated and self-aware, never performative. Sentence rhythms feel like natural thought, including fragments, asides, and self-corrections.

dimensions.label: Sentences vary in length and structure, including fragments and trailing constructions that mimic natural thought
Humor is self-deprecating or observational and never calls attention to itself as a joke
Includes casual asides or parenthetical thoughts that feel like the writer thinking in real time
No line feels like it's trying too hard to be quotable, poetic, or writerly

rubric.name: Sensory Specificity & Authenticity
rubric.description: Sensory details must be specific, unexpected, and personal — the kind only someone who was actually there could write. Generic or atmospheric descriptions fail; the goal is the weird, true detail that makes a reader stop and reread. Comparisons surprise through precision, not poeticism.

dimensions.label: Sensory descriptions go beyond naming ingredients or categories to capture something unexpected or hard to articulate
Comparisons reach into unexpected domains (a sound like a washing machine, a smell like cedar in a sauna) rather than staying within the expected sensory register
At least one sensory detail is anchored to a personal comparison or memory that only this writer would reach for
No sensory language settles for atmospheric but unspecific phrasing — details are precise rather than reaching-for-poetic
Details engage more than one sense and layer them (e.g., smell described through warmth, texture, sharpness)

rubric.name: Section Architecture & Forward Momentum
rubric.description: Sections bridge forward rather than conclude. Endings leave the reader leaning in, not settling back. Transitions between registers — from immersive scene to context or reflection — are anchored in a scene-level moment rather than executed as hard cuts. Sequential observations resonate as variations on a shared idea.

dimensions.label: The section's final beat creates anticipation or lands the reader in an unresolved moment rather than wrapping things up
Shifts in register (immersive to contextual, scene to backstory) are motivated by an in-scene detail, character, or moment — no gear shifts without the clutch
Backstory or contextual details are woven in where they do active work — connecting forward to what comes next, not floating as standalone observations
Sequential observations within a section resonate thematically — they build on a shared idea rather than sitting as an unconnected list
Reflective or philosophical lines don't resolve the section's energy — they open questions rather than answer them

rubric.name: Embodied Immediacy
rubric.description: Writing renders experience as felt in the body, not observed from a distance. The reader should feel the physical jolt — what the mouth does, what the shoulders do, what the jaw does — rather than being told something was powerful. This is the kind of physical detail the user always wants more of.

dimensions.label: At least one moment captures an involuntary physical reaction (mouth watering, feet stopping, shoulders dropping, jaw unlocking)
Intensity is conveyed through physical rendering, not through adjectives or claims about how powerful something was
Key moments unfold with a present-tense, you-are-here quality even if written in past tense
The body's response precedes or overrides conscious thought — experience lands before interpretation

rubric.name: Restraint & Trust in the Reader
rubric.description: The writing stays in the room rather than summarizing the room. Meaning is conveyed through small actions, gestures, and physical details — not through the narrator explaining significance. Endings stay present. Lines that 'wrap a bow on it' or tell the reader what to think are replaced by moments the reader can sit inside.

dimensions.label: Key moments stay in the physical scene — the writing ends in the room, not in the narrator's head reflecting on the room
Any reflective or philosophical line is grounded in a specific preceding experience — it doesn't float
The writing avoids tying things up too tidily — comfortable with ambiguity, not-knowing, or the unexplained
Does not tell the reader how to feel or flag the significance of a moment — lets the detail do the work
Small actions or nonverbal moments carry emotional weight without being explained or annotated

coaching_notes: This user diagnoses problems in their own writing with exceptional precision — they use spatial and cinematic metaphors ('gear shift without the clutch,' 'establishing shot,' 'wrapping a bow on it') that are often more useful than the fixes they request. When they say something 'floats' or 'doesn't land,' the fix is almost always to ground it in a physical detail or connect it forward structurally. Their non-negotiable is that sensory details must be surprising and precise rather than atmospheric — 'ghost of smoke' fails where 'wooden spice drawer with wet earth' succeeds. When stuck, asking 'what do you actually remember?' consistently unlocks the best material.
